In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. == Japan Search name ID (P6698) == * 鵜坂神社 {{commons category|Category:Usaka-jinja}} [[JA:鵜坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11675698}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Usaka Shrine |画像=[[Image:Usaka-jinja_haiden.jpeg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}{{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}Fuchu-machi Usaka 212 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|40|18|N|137|11|12|E|scale:30000}} |祭神=Omodaru-no-Kami
Ayakashikone-no-Kami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}（small）
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） {{DEFAULTSORT:うさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:越中国の式内社]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） {{DEFAULTSORT:うさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:越中国の式内社]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

== Japan Search name ID (P6698) == * 鵜坂神社 {{commons category|Category:Usaka-jinja}} [[JA:鵜坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11675698}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Usaka Shrine |画像=[[Image:Usaka-jinja_haiden.jpeg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}{{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}Fuchu-machi Usaka 212 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|40|18|N|137|11|12|E|scale:30000}} |祭神=Omodaru-no-Kami
Ayakashikone-no-Kami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}（small）
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） {{DEFAULTSORT:うさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:越中国の式内社]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

== Japan Search name ID (P6698) == * 鵜坂神社 {{commons category|Category:Usaka-jinja}} [[JA:鵜坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11675698}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Usaka Shrine |画像=[[Image:Usaka-jinja_haiden.jpeg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}{{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}Fuchu-machi Usaka 212 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|40|18|N|137|11|12|E|scale:30000}} |祭神=Omodaru-no-Kami
Ayakashikone-no-Kami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}（small）
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） {{DEFAULTSORT:うさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:越中国の式内社]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}{{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}Fuchu-machi Usaka 212 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|40|18|N|137|11|12|E|scale:30000}} |祭神=Omodaru-no-Kami
Ayakashikone-no-Kami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}（small）
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami}} *{{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095|1=651}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266|1=Toyama (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami|1=Omodaru and Ayakashikone}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=Toyouke-hime}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami|1=Emperor Ōjin}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} *{{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104|1=652}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518|1=Jinzū River}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676|1=Matsuo Bashō}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326|1=Takarai Kikaku}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War|1=World War II}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234|1=Kokushi (official)}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308|1=Ōtomo no Yakamochi}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） {{DEFAULTSORT:うさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:越中国の式内社]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. category|Category:Usaka-jinja}} [[JA:鵜坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11675698}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Usaka Shrine |画像=[[Image:Usaka-jinja_haiden.jpeg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266|1=Toyama (city)}}Fuchu-machi Usaka 212 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|40|18|N|137|11|12|E|scale:30000}} |祭神=Omodaru-no-Kami
Ayakashikone-no-Kami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095|1=651}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266|1=Toyama (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami|1=Omodaru and Ayakashikone}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=Toyouke-hime}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami|1=Emperor Ōjin}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} *{{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104|1=652}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518|1=Jinzū River}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676|1=Matsuo Bashō}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326|1=Takarai Kikaku}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War|1=World War II}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234|1=Kokushi (official)}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308|1=Ōtomo no Yakamochi}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095|1=651}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266|1=Toyama (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami|1=Omodaru and Ayakashikone}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=Toyouke-hime}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami|1=Emperor Ōjin}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} *{{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104|1=652}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518|1=Jinzū River}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676|1=Matsuo Bashō}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326|1=Takarai Kikaku}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War|1=World War II}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234|1=Kokushi (official)}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308|1=Ōtomo no Yakamochi}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） {{DEFAULTSORT:うさかしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:越中国の式内社]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

category|Category:Usaka-jinja}} [[JA:鵜坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11675698}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Usaka Shrine |画像=[[Image:Usaka-jinja_haiden.jpeg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地={{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266|1=Toyama (city)}}Fuchu-machi Usaka 212 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|40|18|N|137|11|12|E|scale:30000}} |祭神=Omodaru-no-Kami
Ayakashikone-no-Kami |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}（small）
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095|1=651}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266|1=Toyama (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami|1=Omodaru and Ayakashikone}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=Toyouke-hime}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami|1=Emperor Ōjin}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} *{{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104|1=652}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518|1=Jinzū River}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676|1=Matsuo Bashō}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326|1=Takarai Kikaku}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War|1=World War II}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234|1=Kokushi (official)}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308|1=Ōtomo no Yakamochi}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]][[Category:qqqqqqq]]

former {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2（{{ill|lt=651|Q31095|WD=Q31095|1=651}}） |本殿={{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} copper-plate roof |例祭={{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}} |地図= {{Location map|Japan Toyama|label=Usaka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Usaka Shrine'''（うさかじんじゃ）is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Usaka, Fuchu-machi, {{ill|lt=Toyama|Q204266|WD=Q204266|1=Toyama (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Toyama Prefecture|Q132929|WD=Q132929|1=Toyama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinai Shosha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=shikinai small shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine ranking|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === At present, the main deities are {{ill|lt=Omodaru/Ayakashikone|Q11152535|WD=Q11152535|lt=Omodaru-no-Kami and Ayakashikone-no-Kami|1=Omodaru and Ayakashikone}}, with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-hime-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Usaka-tsuma-hiko-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}} as associated deities. Based on the names of the associated deities, a strong theory holds that the deity called "Usaka-no-Kami," matching the shrine's name, was originally the principal deity, and that the associated deities were elder and later deities connected to that main deity. Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=Toyouke-hime}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami|1=Emperor Ōjin}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} *{{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104|1=652}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518|1=Jinzū River}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. Also called the "Buttocks-Striking Festival," it was a rite said to admonish women about chastity. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676|1=Matsuo Bashō}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326|1=Takarai Kikaku}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was changed to a rite of striking a mare's buttocks, and around the end of {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|lt=the Second World War|1=World War II}} it was discontinued. === Facilities === The honden stands with the Jinzū River to its rear, with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} and {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}} placed in front. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234|1=Kokushi (official)}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308|1=Ōtomo no Yakamochi}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument." On the approach there is a stone called "Ibo-ishi" (wart stone), believed to be efficacious for warts and hemorrhoids. For warts, one rubs the affected area; for hemorrhoids, one quietly sits on it. Image:Usaka-jinja_honden.jpeg|Honden Image:Usaka-jinja_entrance.jpeg|Entrance Image:Usaka-jinja_Yakamochi-kahi.jpeg|Usaka River poem monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Basho_kuhi.jpeg|Bashō haiku monument Image:Usaka-jinja_Ibo-ishi.jpeg|Ibo-ishi === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === {{commonscat|Usaka-jinja}} *[http://usaka.jp/usakajinjya/ Usaka Shrine]（Usaka school district regional information portal） == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Usaka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:富山市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Etchū Province]] [[Category:富山県の旧県社]]

Because shrines in former Usaka Village were {{ill|lt=merged shrines|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|lt=consolidated|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the {{ill|lt=Taisho era|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}, the following deities were additionally enshrined: *{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}・{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōkami|1=Toyouke-hime}} *{{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Honda-wake-Ōkami|1=Emperor Ōjin}} *{{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} *{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} *{{ill|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata|Q3081366|WD=Q3081366|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} *{{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} === History === According to shrine tradition, its origin was an enshrinement about 2,000 years ago by {{ill|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|Q11435420|WD=Q11435420|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto}}, the {{ill|lt=Shidō shōgun|Q9076804|WD=Q9076804|lt=Hokurikudō commander|1=Shidō Shōgun}}, in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. It is said to have been formally established in {{ill|lt=652|Q31104|WD=Q31104|1=652}}（{{ill|lt=Hakuchi|Q540118|WD=Q540118|1=Hakuchi (era)}} 2）. In the Heian period it rose to {{ill|lt=court rank for deities|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}{{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} and was revered by the {{ill|lt=Imperial court of Japan|Q30253177|WD=Q30253177|lt=imperial court|1=Imperial Court of Japan}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612|1=Hakuhō}} period, Usaka-dera of the {{ill|lt=Shingon school|Q550182|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} was built and flourished as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. It declined, however, due to repeated damage during the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} and flooding from the nearby {{ill|lt=Jinzū River|Q1156518|WD=Q1156518|1=Jinzū River}}. The temple was abolished in {{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|WD=Q7741|1=1870}} during {{ill|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|Q702298|WD=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}. No temple halls survive. In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=Ken-sha}}. === Festivals === *New Year Festival January 1 *National Foundation Festival February 11 *Prayer for Good Harvest Festival February 23 *Annual Grand Festival April 29 *Insect-Exorcism Festival / Iemochi Akira Festival / Usaka-dera Bettō Memorial Rite June 16 *Niinamesai November 23 From the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, a special ritual called the Nari Festival was held. It derived from a courtly custom claiming that striking women's buttocks with firewood used to cook {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=nanakusa-gayu|1=UNKNOWN}} in the New Year would result in healthy children. As one of the "Five Great Strange Festivals of Japan," it became known nationwide, and was even composed about by {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676|1=Matsuo Bashō}} and {{ill|lt=Takarai Kikaku|Q2118326|WD=Q2118326|1=Takarai Kikaku}}. To the left of the haiden remain gravestones and other traces of Usaka-dera. A waka poem composed when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Etchū|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=provincial governor|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234|1=Kokushi (official)}} {{ill|lt=Ōtomo no Yakamochi|Q386308|WD=Q386308|1=Ōtomo no Yakamochi}} visited this place is carved on the "Usaka River Poem Monument."
